ChatGPT's answers lacked logical rigor, Gemini was too flamboyant and flattering, only Claude, like an objective and professional senior consultant, read through the client's requirements word by word before outputting a truly usable and inspiring solution. After using it for free for more than half a month, Su Yu spent about 180 RMB to subscribe to Claude's monthly membership. Compared to Gemini and ChatGPT, Claude is unique in that it also sets daily and weekly limits on token consumption for its members. This is understandable, as according to LMARaena, a globally renowned blind testing leaderboard for large models, Claude's flagship model, Claude-Opus-4-6-thinking, ranked first globally as of March 20th. However, Su Yu had never experienced such a direct token restriction. The first time she triggered Claude's limit mechanism was on a Wednesday; she was only halfway through understanding "grounded theory" before she could no longer access it, leaving her with a sense of helplessness, a feeling of "academic stagnation." Having become accustomed to Claude's assistance, she found it difficult to return to her initial research mindset. She tried to "manually" consult the most original theoretical books, but the efficiency was extremely low, and she didn't fully trust some translated materials. "In the end, I still had to wait for Claude to resume operation before reviewing everything." The four days of waiting were agonizing. The Claude usage limit was causing Su Yu extreme anxiety. One Tuesday, Su Yu sent a screenshot of her Claude backend, showing that she had already used 45% of her weekly limit. "It's only been two days this week! I've been using it very sparingly, only discussing one paper topic a day, and it's already reached the limit!" Su Yu was somewhat distraught. Who says AI can't replace humans? This AI is almost more difficult to deal with than her supervisor. [Image of Su Yu's Claude backend] Image source: Interviewee

She has developed the habit of checking the backend every time she asks a question, afraid of running out of ideas. Thinking back to how she used to chat with Claude and have him help her create PPTs, she inwardly scolded herself for wasting time.

This cautious use of "good models" is gradually becoming common.An AI film and television industry entrepreneur told me that when his team uses ByteDance's AI video model "Jimeng," they simultaneously access the APIs of several other model vendors. "The better models are indeed more expensive, so we can only switch between different models to balance costs."

Not long ago, Jimeng lowered its membership points limit, which he felt was normal, "The C-end was already subsidized; now it's just taking back a portion."

On the other hand, they worry about their own situation, lamenting, "Now it's even more unaffordable." Rising AI costs can sometimes directly jeopardize the lifeline of small startups. End users are anxious about tokens, and model manufacturers are also anxious about computing power costs. Regarding the reasons for the surge in token usage, Wang Jian, an academician of the Chinese Academy of Engineering, previously used the development of electricity as an analogy: early AI applications were like "lighting up a lamp," consuming limited electricity. However, the new generation of applications, represented by OpenClaw (intelligent agents), are like turning on an "air conditioner," requiring increasingly more electricity. However, Wang Jian emphasized that this growth not only signifies the widespread adoption of applications but also the decrease in the cost of individual tokens. "If electricity prices don't drop, ordinary people won't be able to afford air conditioning." However, compared to the simple question-and-answer calls of the early days, more and more tasks are now completed through agents. Models need to break down problems, call tools, write code, debug, and then correct them. A seemingly simple request often corresponds to multiple rounds of inference and multiple API calls, exponentially increasing token consumption. Although the unit price has decreased, the overall computing power cost is higher. "As the model has become larger, the inference cost has also increased accordingly. We also hope to bring it back to its normal commercial value. Long-term reliance on low-price competition is not conducive to the development of the entire industry, which is also one of our considerations," said Zhang Peng, CEO of Zhipu. In the past two months, Zhipu has raised the price of its GLM (Zhipu's large language model) series models three times, with some model prices approaching the pricing level of leading international models. Zhang Peng's other concern is that "the biggest problem we face in the next 12 months may be computing power. All technologies, including intelligent agent frameworks, have increased the creativity and efficiency of many people by 10 times. But the prerequisite is that everyone can use them. We can't let insufficient computing power cause an agent to think for a long time without giving me an answer." According to Claude's calculation method, 100 Tokens are roughly equivalent to 75 English words or 50 Chinese characters, and the price of the output of a Token is five times the price of the input—this is a very simple conversion. In other words, every answer from AI must be carefully considered. The tokens consumed by the background thinking, querying, generation, and even errors caused by model illusions will all be calculated and ultimately become a real-money bill. Lin Zhijia, founder of AGI (AI Encoding Intelligence), did the math. He raised four "lobsters" (AI tokens), some deployed locally and some in the cloud. Taking the cloud deployment as an example, he purchased a monthly Coding Plan (AI coding subscription service) for about 30-40 RMB. With nine days left in March, his token consumption was less than 10% of the plan's limit—as a media professional, his need for tokens was actually not significant. However, billing by tokens wasn't cost-effective. “If I just have it send me a news message at 9 AM every day, the tokens it consumes cost about 0.9 RMB, which is about 20 RMB over 30 days, almost the same as the cost of a Coding Plan. Sometimes there are also losses and model updates, which can consume three or four RMB worth of tokens just for updates.” Weighing the pros and cons between different billing methods has become a daily routine for frequent users, and every penny spent on purchasing tokens ultimately points to the same thing—computing power, and the corresponding GPU depreciation costs and data center power consumption. GPUs have become the starting point for everything; the supply of high-end chips determines the upper limit of the entire system. “Except for the backup machines reserved for some customers, we've basically sold out; not a single card is left,” said Liu Hua, Deputy General Manager of UCloud's Architecture Technology Center. Below the GPUs, data centers, networks, and storage systems must also be built—high-speed interconnection and low-latency transmission are not "plug-and-play" standard components. Liu Hua mentioned that the cost of network and storage alone can account for about 20% of the total computing power cost. Next, there are model vendors and API service providers. They deploy large models on this infrastructure, encapsulating them into standardized interfaces for developers to use. In the past two years, these roles have begun to overlap, with cloud vendors selling computing power and providing model APIs, gradually becoming the central hub connecting GPUs, models, and developers.

● A diagram illustrating how computing power flows. Image source: AI generated

Computing power thus penetrates downwards layer by layer, with the latest change occurring on the demand side of industries. “Previously, AI was mostly paid for by B2B companies, but now it’s becoming increasingly common for B2C companies to pay,” said Lin Zhijia. Models are encapsulated into APIs, the entry point is simplified, and the barrier to entry is lowered, allowing individual developers and even ordinary users to directly access the underlying computing power. “Now, basically everyone just needs to browse social media to know how to use it.” Computing power is even showing a trend towards retailization. Around 2024, some cloud vendors began launching GPU “daily cards,” lightweight cloud servers, and even “one-click deployment” trial products. For example, UCloud’s 6.9 yuan trial package for “shrimp farmers” is essentially more like a ticket, packaging complex environment configuration and computing power scheduling, allowing users to try it out at a very low cost. “Many people are actually here to ‘test the waters’ or try something new,” Liu Hua said. “Everyone is a little anxious, afraid of falling behind.” But lower barriers to entry do not mean lower costs. In Liu Hua’s view, “Using the analogy of the development stages of the Internet, the current cost of computing power is undoubtedly still in an early stage of development, a very expensive stage.” This is why developers are careful with their spending, and platforms are hesitant to easily expand the scale of resource usage. Even leading companies are making trade-offs. OpenAI's previous shutdown of its video generation project, Sora, was interpreted by many industry insiders as a trade-off between computing power and return on investment. With limited resources, they prioritized focusing on more core model capabilities and business operations. The recent adjustments made to AI businesses by internet giants like Alibaba, Tencent, and ByteDance also reflect a focus on concentrating computing resources. Everyone is realizing one thing: the future competition will not be about the scale of computing power, but about the efficiency of computing power utilization. The chain reaction caused by the shortage of computing power is like a long rainy season in the AI ​​era, and everyone living in this era will inevitably experience its challenges. What happens when computing power flows to the end? Su Yu is trying to allocate and schedule computing resources. She has divided different models into layers: ChatGPT is used for writing official documents and compiling briefings, Gemini is responsible for drawing diagrams and handling language details, and Claude is used specifically for the most critical aspects, such as research frameworks, designing ideas, and analyzing long texts. This ensures that both her efficiency and budget are maximized. For example, she recently processed a batch of interview materials, first having Claude provide an analysis framework, and then "throwing" this framework to Gemini for initial coding. “I trust Claude’s guidance more, but I can delegate the detailed work to cheaper models.” If Claude didn’t have a limit, she would even stop using Gemini. Of course, this isn’t an advertisement for Claude; it’s just that Su Yu believes this application is more suitable for her needs. Good models are becoming scarce, and scarce resources are only used where they are most critical. To further save money, many users, like Su Yu, have started cutting costs on the details. On social media, it became popular to use classical Chinese to converse with AI, because shorter characters meant fewer tokens. Some argue that blindly following the trend of saying “hello” and “thank you” to AI is also an unnecessary waste of resources. After all, AI doesn’t need emotional value. In fact, much waste is not within the user's control; sometimes it's a problem with the model's access and operation. Not long ago, Luo Fuli, head of the MiMo large model team, mentioned, "I can't strictly calculate the losses caused by third-party harness integration, but I've seen OpenClaw's context management up close, and it's terrible. In a single user query, it triggers multiple rounds of low-value tool calls, each round issued as an independent API request, and each request often carries a context window exceeding 100K tokens. The actual number of requests is several times that of the native Claude Code framework. Converted to API pricing, the real cost is probably dozens of times the subscription price." Returning to the issue of usage, users actively conserve tokens, and platforms dare not fully unleash their user base. This cost-saving "constraint" presents a contradiction, as OpenAI is currently facing—it generated $4.3 billion in revenue in the first half of 2025, while during the same period, the company suffered a net loss of $13.5 billion, meaning that for every dollar OpenAI earns, it loses three dollars. The bulk of the losses comes from investments in computing power. Currently, computing power is no longer just a matter of availability, but rather the sustainability and extent of its use. When AI becomes sufficiently useful, people will reorganize their work methods around it; when tokens become expensive and limited, this new organizational structure itself will be forced to shrink. If computing power cannot truly become as ubiquitous as electricity in the future, AI will inevitably lead to differentiation, further widening the cognitive gap between people. For example, Su Yu doesn't intend to fully share her AI usage methods with those around her. How to interact with Professor Claude and what kind of data to feed him are her little secrets, and in the short term, they are her competitive advantage. If a colleague asks her to recommend a good model, she strongly recommends Gemini and ChatGPT, "Of course, DeepSeek is also a good choice." Su Yu winked mischievously. In an era where "one-person companies (OPCs)" and "super-individuals" are gaining popularity, such subtle tactics are not uncommon. When the usability of AI is correlated with quantifiable tokens, the real difference lies in how people use it. (Su Yu is a pseudonym in this article) Cover Source: "Cosmic Exploration Editorial Department" References: Emerging Intelligence: "Yang Zhilin/Zhang Peng/Xia Lixue/Luo Fuli/Huang Chao, Talking about Lobsters and 'Token Economics'" Daily Economic News: "AI Drives Massive Token Consumption and Memory Hardware Shortage" Amid the computing power leasing boom, operators are ramping up their liquid-cooled server deployments. (Related articles: "Zhipu's Zhang Peng: When the model is strong enough, the API itself is the best business model"; "Zhipu's stock price hits a record high, and the price of its new generation model increases by another 10%"; "Deep Tide TechFlow: Tokens go global, selling Chinese electricity to the world"; "Silicon Star Pro: Luo Fuli: Wake up, everyone, it's time to end the token frenzy").

data.title: Interview with He Yi: I don't recommend selling your house to go all in on crypto; survival is the priority in the crypto world.
data.description:
Note: This article is a dialogue between Binance Co-CEO He Yi and Du Yu, head of Wanxiang Blockchain Labs, at the Web3 Carnival in Hong Kong on April 26.

Du Yu: Hello everyone! I am Du Yu from Wanxiang Blockchain Labs. I feel your enthusiasm today. No need for introductions, please say hello to everyone.

He Yi: First of all, thank you very much for coming to Hong Kong to see me. Actually, I'm here to see you all too. I'm very happy to see you all here. Hello everyone! I am Xiao He, Binance's "Chief Customer Service Officer." Thank you!

Du Yu: It's an honor to have you here at the event. We also had a dialogue on this stage last year, when you were online. It's a great honor to have you here today.

Du Yu: It's an honor to have you here today. This past year has seen many interesting developments in the industry, especially the impact and opportunities brought by AI. I feel it's a pivotal moment of diverse convergence, and having this dialogue with you here is particularly interesting. In this complex cycle, I saw many people asking you specific questions at yesterday's AMA. Today, I'd like to talk more about your perspective on the industry. Because there are people from various fields here—traditional finance, startups—and I heard many students flew in specifically to hear your speech. In the market environment of the past six months, what are your personal interests? What new changes in your schedule, or what you think about most, excite you or even feel a sense of urgency about? He Yi: Regarding AI, as mentioned earlier, my personal life and time allocation, particularly my work, can be roughly divided into three modules: First, the approaching singularity of AI is indeed a moment that no one can ignore; it truly is a moment in human history. Therefore, I spend a lot of time thinking about the potential global transformation brought about by AI and the blockchain industry, and by AI and finance, and what the future of finance will look like. This means I need to think about what Binance's products and services will likely look like ten years from now. Second, I think I spend a lot of time thinking about organization and talent. Often we say that in the early stages of a company, growth may be driven by the founder, co-founder, or individual strengths. However, when a company reaches a certain size, it becomes about organizational effectiveness—how to enable the organization to grow independently and autonomously. This is something I've been constantly thinking about and working on. Third, people often talk about the further integration between cryptocurrencies and traditional finance. Because of this special moment, we've seen both the US and Hong Kong governments showing tremendous support and encouragement for the entire blockchain industry. Therefore, I think we might see a revolutionary change in the industry in the future. Often, when the singularity arrives, when the moment arrives, we are often unaware. It might happen on an ordinary day, but the changes it brings to our lives could mean that all global foreign exchange transactions will take place on the blockchain, no longer the old SWIFT system. We might see all asset transactions no longer limited to a few hours, but could be conducted globally 24/7, like in the cryptocurrency world. Therefore, we will see the whole world rapidly entering a state of "push-back" momentum. Du Yu: You just described a very exciting future. Often when discussing the future, it seems like we're talking about things three, five, or ten years from now, but this year feels different. I think an exponential inflection point is right there, possibly happening today or tomorrow. I think many of you here probably feel this sense of urgency. Following what you just said about AI and the future of finance, let's dive in and discuss some interesting things. Let's start with AI again. Organization is very important in AI, especially for a company like Binance that's almost ten years old. You've probably seen many tech companies laying off employees recently, but their stock prices haven't dropped. It's not because of poor performance, but because of organizational improvements. There's even been a lot of talk about one-person companies lately, but I personally think that's a bit of an exaggeration or we haven't reached that point yet. However, it's certain that companies with three to five or even ten people can do what companies with dozens or hundreds of people used to do. This is already happening. AI is rewriting everything. How has AI changed your personal thinking and decision-making, the entire Binance platform, and even multinational operations? I think Binance faces more challenges than many other large companies because you are a very global company, and you don't have many customers who are more global or distributed than you. What opportunities and challenges has AI brought you? He Yi: First of all, Binance does not plan to conduct large-scale layoffs or cost-cutting and efficiency-enhancing operations like most tech companies. For us, AI is actually about making strong people even stronger. For example, in the past, many people might have thought that a programmer might have innovative and structured thinking when writing code, but their coding skills might be limited. However, today, when AI-powered coding is already very smooth and mature, it can actually improve efficiency by 10 times. I've discovered something quite interesting: we once discovered that an employee was working multiple jobs, and it was because of AI-powered coding that their efficiency improved significantly. At this point, he would feel that the work was easy to handle, so he would take on another job. Of course, he was eventually fired. The overall thinking is to further improve the organization's productivity and innovation capabilities, rather than simply pursuing cost reduction and efficiency improvement at the existing scale. This year, I proposed an idea that others might think is a bit crazy. In the past, we said we wanted to pursue 1 billion users, but this year I told everyone that we should aim for 3 billion users, because we already have 300 million users. What does becoming a company with 3 billion users mean? It means that Binance is not just an exchange; Binance is a global financial infrastructure. It means that our services should be geared towards ordinary people, towards the masses, towards everyone. These services could be daily payments, daily financial management, or every detail of their daily lives. This will push me to encourage employees to be more innovative and try new things. As their business expands its boundaries, their infrastructure needs to be sufficiently stable. I've received many complaints from friends that our product could be made smoother, so I've spent a lot of time optimizing it this year. Give me a few more months, and I'll make it even better. Secondly, regarding the impact of AI on us, I think the biggest impact is that in the past, when we understood AI, we thought of it as a very ambitious child. But if you have a young child at home, you'll find that children sometimes talk nonsense. I don't know if you've seen the movie "A Little Red Flower," directed by Zhang Yuan, where kindergarten children say their teacher is a monster. You'll find that AI and young, highly intelligent children have very similarities. They may not have structured logic; their memories may be discontinuous. In this logic, we've seen a huge leap. The AI ​​of the previous cycle (2025) was actually a highly intelligent brain, but in just a few months today, I feel that AI has already grown its own hands. For example, the lobster that was popular a while ago, and now these Skills, most of the top-tier models have already been equipped with it. This means that AI is no longer just a concept, but has truly begun to enter everyone's lives. Of course, the most obvious example is probably writing code. Why writing code? Essentially because programmers are currently among the highest-paid people in the world. When people talk about how to use AI, the first step is to replace the highest-paid group of people. So I think that in the longer term, today's work is a bit like the workers in the candle factory of the past, but electricity has arrived, and electric lights have arrived. The huge impact and reflection it may bring us is not just about the old paradigm, but about what kind of responsibility you, as an individual or as a company, bear in the whole society and the whole organization when new technological changes arrive. But I don't think this is unusual for individual companies. If you look back at the era of internet celebrities, personal IPs, such as the previous livestreamer Li Jiaqi, can achieve a scale of hundreds of millions, much larger than many companies. But I think the greater reflection is on what part of the responsibility you bear for the public in the whole society and the whole organization. Du Yu: You mentioned earlier that you're aiming to grow from 300 million users to 3 billion users. Actually, when I was preparing for the questions, including when you spoke, my opinion was that you were too conservative. Why? With the development of AI, we ourselves feel that Crypto itself isn't for humans, but for AI. Many people say that in the future, whether you're wealthy will depend on how many robots you have, how much AI you have, and how much computing power you can mobilize. So, have you considered that in Binance's product design, and not just Binance, but the entire Web3 industry, the product design of the entrepreneurs here is geared towards AI users and AI agents? At that point, the user base won't be 300 million or 3 billion, but potentially tens or hundreds of billions of users. The architecture will be different then, and the opportunities it brings to Web3 will be different. Standing here today, it feels like all the traffic and capital have been attracted to AI, but what still excites me is the combination of AI with Web3, and then with robots and robot dogs. This is truly the next generation of the incredibly exciting internet. What are your thoughts on this trend? He Yi: I think AI has entered two different branches, of course there are conflicts and discussions. For example, the path from large language models to Web Coding is one, while physical AI has actually made many breakthroughs. If we must look to the future, you shouldn't see Crypto as just Crypto, but as a more efficient and lower-cost financial system. On such a highly optimized structure, how can AI serve the public more efficiently? A while ago there was a discussion on Twitter where people said that in the West, Anthropic and OpenAI made short videos satirizing each other, while China's AI war was just about delivering milk tea. This question made me think for a long time. I think we shouldn't be technologically arrogant. No matter how amazing or advanced AI technology is, for individuals, for human beings, the most important thing is how this technology serves me and helps improve my life. Whether it's Doubao or Qianwen, when it can tell an elderly woman in rural China that a third-party recommended health product is fake and she shouldn't buy it, I think that's the value and meaning that technology brings to humanity. When a lonely elderly person in a rural area can have someone to chat with every day through Doubao, I think that's the meaning that technology brings to humanity. No matter where humanity goes, we must still remember that we are human, and we must still cherish the green grass and trees.

Du Yu: Especially for people like us with a technical background, sometimes there's an instinct to show off. Many Web3 startups and companies might think it's something very cool, a very cool product, a very cool technology, but in the end, they find there are no users, or they might only be serving a very narrow user base. You're always called "customer service" or "first customer service," but you're also the first product manager. It's very important to understand human nature. All technological advancements, including advancements in financial tools, are for serving better humanity. We are not ultimately serving robots; robots also serve people, and AI is also used to serve people.

... Earlier you mentioned financial infrastructure, and I also believe that the core of Web3 is financial infrastructure, or the next generation of financial infrastructure. A while ago I saw you retweet Binance's Twitter post, which featured two pie charts: Crypto on the left, traditional Trade-Fi on the right, and Binance at the intersection. I think when I first encountered Binance in 2017, I thought the name was really good. I don't know how you came up with that name back then; you wrote about it in your book "My Binance Life," but it seemed to be written rather simply. As I understand it, Finance is easy to understand—it represents existing finance—but B could be Bitcoin, or the entire digital world. In this overlapping or intersecting process, why do you think Binance is iterating and overlapping? What do you think Binance wants to express in the future financial system? He Yi: Back in 2014, the slogan was "Down with Wall Street." Today, as your industry continues to expand, with more and more institutional users and traditional finance embracing crypto, you'll find that this is actually a process of integration. In this integration process, some countries and some regulators are ahead, saying, "I understand what it is, I understand its significance to the financial system." Today, it might be about letting some people try and innovate first. In Hong Kong, companies like Futu and Tiger have already started simultaneously holding equity assets and crypto assets. In the US, companies like Robinhood also have a lot of crypto assets and traditional equity assets. Binance may not just want to tie itself to crypto or so-called equity assets. We recently launched some commodities, including crude oil, gold, and silver. Our platform's commodity trading volume already accounts for a very high proportion of the traditional market. They might now see us as a threat, and they might also shout "Down with Crypto!" But in the longer term, when you want to become part of the financial infrastructure, you don't need to worry too much about whether you're a Crypto or a Trade File, because when we label ourselves, that labeling is a process of self-imposed limitations. When you break through those boundaries and return to the starting point, it's about what you've done for financial freedom in the world, what you've done for financial equality, and whether you've made life better for more ordinary people—that's a very fundamental starting point. Du Yu: You mentioned earlier that the fundamental starting point is very good and important, but sometimes it can be idealistic. Because when we actually do things, there are always compromises with reality and regulation. My question can be approached from two perspectives. First, many traditional mainstream financial institutions are embracing Crypto today, including the two stablecoin licenses issued in Hong Kong last week, which are held by mainstream financial institutions and banks. But sometimes we have doubts. Mainstream financial institutions are already doing well enough; for them, it's just a small innovation that can succeed or fail. Even making stablecoins might negatively impact their revenue because they have to invest, and investment doesn't guarantee success.

From another perspective, from the outsider's viewpoint, that of a crypto native outside the crypto circle. Initially, when people talked about the ups and downs of Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies, I joined Wanxiang Blockchain in 2015. For over a decade, I always felt embarrassed to tell my friends I worked in blockchain every three or four years because the industry was often stigmatized. Even if I said "blockchain" and we were doing technical work, people would think I was just speculating on cryptocurrencies, and they'd label me with a lot of tags. Things have improved a lot in recent years; talking about Bitcoin now brings with it negative associations.

But with the development over the years, crypto native companies are also facing regulation and are becoming more mainstream. From your perspective, which path does Binance prefer, or is it difficult for Binance, which originated from the crypto native world, to follow? How do you think it's more practical to achieve the vision of first principles step by step?

He Yi: The path we're on today is the same one the internet has already traversed. When the internet first emerged in Silicon Valley, there was a strong "hippie culture." Back then, many people thought those involved in the internet and technology were geeks, weirdos. But in that era, the internet branched out dramatically. Those who went left created the dark web, enjoying absolute freedom; those who went right created Google and Amazon, becoming ordinary businesses serving a wider global audience. If you ask me where Binance is headed, we will definitely remain steadfast in our commitment to becoming a business serving the masses. But at the same time, while serving the masses, technological innovation is still extremely important, whether it's blockchain technology or AI technology. Often, people think the stigma and public opinion surrounding cryptocurrencies are normal because if everyone could understand new things, and there were no controversies, how could there be so much hardship, challenges, and continuous innovation? Looking back at my childhood when I went to internet cafes, my mother probably wanted to break my legs. When we see blockchain being embraced by Wall Street and traditional finance in a complete 180-degree turn, it precisely indicates that the industry is becoming increasingly mature, and the early-stage dividends are diminishing. When early dividends decrease, complaints tend to increase. For example, people wonder why Bitcoin isn't falling further, making it impossible to buy at the bottom. Why isn't Bitcoin constantly rising? That's because the early-stage dividend period is over. Once that period is over, the industry enters its so-called maturity stage, and crossing that chasm means ensuring all products have real value and practical applications. I have great respect for many professional technical experts in the industry, but I often ask them a question: What is your technology used for? What problems can it solve? Will anyone be willing to pay for it? I remember discussing this topic in a similar setting last year, and the essence of business remains consistent. However, I believe that technological innovation and continuous iteration are endless. Du Yu: Over the past two years, the focus in the industry has shifted away from technology itself; in fact, technology has become a bottleneck to some extent. Current technology is sufficient for most businesses. What we need to focus on is researching existing technologies. Web3 has many Layer 1 and Layer 2 technologies; the key is how to utilize them, how to change people's lives, and how to gradually build applications. You also mentioned that this industry is gradually entering the mainstream. This year's Hong Kong Web3 Festival, and what we've been doing for the past few years, is attracting more mainstream industries to use Web3 and crypto as valuable tools to help them advance further, just as the internet transformed almost every industry. I believe that Web3, as a new financial infrastructure, will also significantly improve all existing business models. Finally, I have a little time left, and I'd like to talk about some things mentioned in CZ's recent book, *Binance Life*. This book allows more people to understand his journey, especially from his entry into the industry to the present. His growth story is the growth story of Binance, and even more so, the growth story of the entire industry. He wrote many stories in the book, and today we'd like to hear your perspective. How would you summarize it from another perspective as a firsthand witness, or if we push the timeline forward ten or twenty years, how would you recount the events of those ten-plus years? A very important entrepreneurial journey. He Yi: If we go back to 2017, if you look at my WeChat Moments, you'll find that from 2017 until now, I've been posting the phrase, "We're not just starting a business, we're creating history." So, whether it's the past, present, or future, what we're truly doing is figuring out how to use technology to change the future, how to create history. In the course of history, every path you take is naturally not a straight line, not a smooth one. Whether it's the so-called stigmatization of the cryptocurrency world, calling people "you bunch of stinky crypto speculators," or the public's recognition of technological innovation, these are all part of the journey you must take responsibility for. You can't simply understand life as a smooth, uninterrupted journey. If you choose to be a history maker, you also need to bear historical responsibility. This is why, ten years from now, when I describe Binance, I will still say, "We are not starting a business, we are making history." Our slogan has never changed; it has always been "Exchange The World," not just the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange. When you look at it from a longer historical perspective, you'll find that our current discussions about AI's supposed drain on cryptocurrencies, or the impact of AI on cryptocurrencies, are actually normal. The impact of AI isn't just on Web3, or just on cryptocurrencies; it's ubiquitous and inevitable. However, the impact and transformation of Web3 on the entire financial sector is real. Many people will say that all this Web3 stuff is still just trading and finance, and isn't finance a huge industry? If it can improve the efficiency of all finance even a little bit, that's already a remarkable innovation. There's no need to pursue instant success; just take it one step at a time and do a little bit well every day. Du Yu: The initial intention is very important. Some people go astray when making history. You'll find that very few people in this industry can truly persevere for more than ten years. In the book *Binance Life*, I saw many frequently used words; a media outlet even listed them. "Customer," "integrity," etc., truly reflect a heartfelt desire to do one thing well, and this permeates the entire book. Only when your initial intention is correct, and you persist in doing the right thing—perhaps difficult, but the right thing—will you achieve great success after five, ten, or even longer years. He Yi: When doing something difficult but right, people often say, "You make it sound so good, but you're not saints." We're certainly not saints, but for everyone to achieve a win-win situation, what you need to do is, as your platform grows and you have more users, the industry expands, and everyone benefits. Being at the top of the industry naturally makes you a company with greater commercial and public value. I don't believe in purely zero-sum games. I believe that when what you do has value for society, and people pay for it, people are willing to support you, then earning your due is enough. This process is definitely a spiral upward process, accompanied by controversy and pain, but only a small group of people, after achieving financial freedom, will say, "I want to see how far I can go." Du Yu: Many of you here today are already Web3 practitioners, and there's an interesting phenomenon: many of our attendees come from diverse backgrounds. Many have heard of Web3 but haven't actually worked in the industry. We see a lot of people from mainstream, traditional industries. If you were to share a truly insightful observation that only those who have experienced it firsthand could understand, with these guests who are just starting out, considering entering the field, or are simply interested in Web3, what would you say? What advice would you offer? He Yi: Let's get down to business. Many people enter the crypto world because someone tells them a coin has increased tenfold and they should buy it. Traditional Buffett's value investing theory is quite applicable to any industry. If you don't understand an industry, buy top-tier assets. You don't need many choices; just select a few top-tier assets as a starting point to understand the industry. I often say that Bitcoin is the largest decentralized asset, and BNB is the largest centralized asset. Based on this logic, a small allocation is appropriate. Many friends say they came to the crypto world to "take a gamble and turn a bicycle into a motorcycle." In that case, risk management is crucial. I often tell them not to be afraid, but to understand whether their decisions are a revolving door or a one-way door. A revolving door means that even if you fail, you can return to where you started, either gaining something or learning something. But some decisions are irreversible; there's no going back. Be cautious about those. For example, I really don't recommend the kind of all-in approach like selling your house to invest in computer vision. Du Yu: The best way to understand an industry or a company is to buy its stock. I also tell my friends to try it out themselves to understand. He Yi: Allocate small amounts, do more research, and act less. Du Yu: AI is the same; you have to use it first. It's not that complex. He Yi: One is called execution, and the other is cognition. You just need to iterate your cognition during the execution process, but you must understand whether your input costs are controllable. Du Yu: Due to time constraints, thank you, Sister Yi. Our conversation with you will end here. We look forward to having a more interesting and in-depth discussion with you on the same stage next year. Thank you, Sister Yi, and thank you everyone! He Yi: Thank you everyone!

data.title: Coinbase Tests AI Agents Fred And Balaji To Help Employees With Strategy, Creativity And Daily Work Tasks
data.description:
AI Agents Enter Coinbase Workflows Through Slack And Email

Coinbase is bringing artificial intelligence deeper into its day-to-day operations, with AI agents now being tested inside workplace tools such as Slack and email.

The system is designed to let employees interact with AI in the same way they would with human colleagues, using it for support in tasks ranging from planning to execution.

Brian Armstrong, CEO of Coinbase, explained on X that the company has already introduced two early agents.

“Coinbase is testing AI agents that show up in Slack/email at work, just like any human teammate. To start, we’re shipping two, which are modeled after legendary former Coinbase employees, Fred Ersham and Balaji Srinivasan.”

He added that the rollout is still in its early stages but expected to expand quickly across teams.

Coinbase is testing AI agents that show up in slack/email at work, just like any human teammate. To start we're shipping two which are modeled after legendary former Coinbase employees, @FEhrsam and @balajis. (Who brutally frame mogged who in this matchup?)

Soon, it will be easy… pic.twitter.com/1bxfh8Dg9q

— Brian Armstrong (@brian_armstrong) April 18, 2026

Fred And Balaji Take On Strategic And Creative Roles

The two internal AI agents have been designed with distinct functions based on former Coinbase executives.

Fred, named after co-founder Fred Ehrsam, acts as a strategic executive-style assistant.

It helps staff refine ideas, review plans, and improve decision-making by offering structured feedback and priority alignment.

Balaji, inspired by former CTO Balaji Srinivasan, plays a very different role.

Described as an “agent of chaos and creativity”, it is intended to push teams beyond conventional thinking, challenge assumptions, and support long-term innovation projects.

Armstrong said,

“I think these employee agents should have their own name actually. Not a ‘digital twin’ of someone else. So that is next step.”

He suggested that future versions could evolve into standalone identities rather than simply reflecting real individuals.

Will AI Agents Outnumber Human Employees At Coinbase?

Armstrong has pointed to a future where AI could become a major part of the workforce structure.

“Soon, it will be easy for any employee to spin up a new agent for themselves or their team. I suspect we will have more agents than human employees at some point soon.”

The company has also indicated broader ambitions to transform its workforce into “AI-Natives”, with automation increasingly embedded into everyday operations.

Last month, Armstrong shared that “more than 50% of our (Coinbase) code is being written by AI Agents,” while “customer support is more than 60% answered by AI.”

AI agents at @Coinbase now write over 50% of all code and handle 60% of customer support tickets.@Brian_Armstrong just revealed Coinbase is treating AI agents like actual digital employees.

Complete with their own stablecoin wallets.

These agents can work overnight, spin up… https://t.co/WSRxzNtN95pic.twitter.com/goawHY6mZG

— Milk Road (@MilkRoad) March 2, 2026

From Internal Tools To Blockchain-Powered Agent Economy

The move fits into Coinbase’s wider push into what the industry is calling an “agent economy”, where autonomous systems can interact financially on-chain.

The firm has already launched the x402 protocol, designed to enable AI agents to make payments across both crypto and fiat rails.

🔥COINBASE UNVEILS X402 PROTOCOL: UNIVERSAL STANDARD EMBEDDING PAYMENTS INTO WEB INTERACTIONS@Coinbase, @Stripe, and @Cloudflare have launched the x402 Foundation under the @linuxfoundation to establish a universal open standard for payments over HTTP.

This new protocol… pic.twitter.com/DE0i6vGxyh

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 2, 2026

It is also positioning its Base layer-2 network as infrastructure for machine-to-machine transactions, including payments for services like data and computing power.

Industry figures have echoed similar expectations, with predictions that AI agents could soon handle a large share of online transactions and become regular users of blockchain networks for payments, trading, and digital services.
